text: What does db.books.insertOne({ title: 'Dune', price: 14.99 }) return on success?
explanation: insertOne() returns an InsertOneResult object with acknowledged (was the write confirmed?) and insertedId (the _id of the new document).
hint: It tells you the write was acknowledged and gives you back the new document's ID.
sqlEquivalent: INSERT INTO books (title, price) VALUES ('Dune', 14.99); — returns row count in SQL

text: What does findOne() return when no document matches the filter?
explanation: findOne() returns null when no document matches. This is different from find() which always returns a cursor (even an empty one). Always check for null before using the result.
hint: It returns something that means 'nothing found' — not an empty array.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT ... WHERE ... LIMIT 1 — returns 0 rows in SQL

text: You run: db.books.updateOne({ title: '1984' }, { price: 20 }). What happens?
options: The price field is updated to 20
The entire document is replaced with { price: 20 }
An error is thrown because you must use an update operator
The update is ignored since there's no $set
correctAnswer: The entire document is replaced with { price: 20 }
explanation: Without an update operator like $set, MongoDB replaces the entire document with the provided object. The result is { _id: ObjectId(...), price: 20 } — all other fields are lost! Always use $set to update specific fields.
hint: Without $set, MongoDB treats the second argument as the complete replacement document.
sqlEquivalent: This would be like UPDATE books SET * = { price: 20 } which is invalid SQL — SQL always requires you to name specific columns

text: Complete this query to delete the first book with the title 'Animal Farm':
explanation: deleteOne() removes the first matching document. If you want to remove all matching documents, use deleteMany().
hint: It starts with 'delete' and ends with 'One'.
sqlEquivalent: DELETE FROM books WHERE title = 'Animal Farm' LIMIT 1

text: Complete this query to find all books where inStock is true:
explanation: find() accepts a filter object as the first argument. Passing { inStock: true } returns all documents where the inStock field equals true.
hint: The read method that returns multiple documents.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT * FROM books WHERE in_stock = true

text: Which update operator increments a numeric field by a given amount?
explanation: $inc increments (or decrements, with a negative value) a numeric field. Example: { $inc: { price: 5 } } adds 5 to the price. { $inc: { price: -5 } } subtracts 5.
hint: It's short for 'increment'.
sqlEquivalent: UPDATE books SET price = price + 5

text: What does db.books.insertMany([doc1, doc2, doc3]) return?
options: { acknowledged: true, insertedIds: { '0': ObjectId, '1': ObjectId, '2': ObjectId } }
An array of the inserted documents
correctAnswer: { acknowledged: true, insertedIds: { '0': ObjectId, '1': ObjectId, '2': ObjectId } }
explanation: insertMany() returns an InsertManyResult with acknowledged and insertedIds — an object mapping the position index to the _id of each inserted document.
hint: Similar to insertOne but with multiple IDs.

text: How do you sort find() results by price in descending order?
explanation: In MongoDB sort, 1 = ascending (ASC) and -1 = descending (DESC). Chain .sort() after find().
hint: -1 = descending, 1 = ascending.
sqlEquivalent: ORDER BY price DESC

text: What is the purpose of the second argument to find() — db.books.find(filter, projection)?
options: Which fields to include or exclude in results
The maximum number of documents to return
correctAnswer: Which fields to include or exclude in results
explanation: The projection object controls which fields are returned. Use 1 to include, 0 to exclude. Example: { title: 1, price: 1, _id: 0 } returns only title and price, without _id.
hint: Like SELECT specific columns in SQL.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT title, price FROM books

text: Which update operator removes a field from a document entirely?
explanation: $unset removes the field entirely from the document. Usage: { $unset: { fieldName: '' } } — the empty string value is ignored; any value works.
hint: It 'un-sets' a field — makes it not exist.
sqlEquivalent: There's no SQL equivalent — closest is UPDATE books SET promo_code = NULL

text: You call insertMany([doc1, doc2, doc3], { ordered: false }). doc2 fails due to a duplicate key. What happens?
options: All three inserts are rolled back
doc1 succeeds, doc2 fails, doc3 is skipped
doc1 succeeds, doc2 fails, doc3 still attempts to insert
An error is thrown and nothing is inserted
correctAnswer: doc1 succeeds, doc2 fails, doc3 still attempts to insert
explanation: With { ordered: false }, MongoDB continues inserting remaining documents even if one fails. Without this option (default ordered: true), insertion stops at the first error.
hint: ordered: false means 'don't stop on error'.
sqlEquivalent: No direct SQL equivalent — PostgreSQL always rolls back the whole INSERT on error

text: Update the price of ALL books to add a $2 increase. Fill in the blanks:
explanation: updateMany({}, ...) matches all documents (empty filter = match all). $inc: { price: 2 } adds 2 to the current price of every book.
hint: Use updateMany for all docs, $inc to add to a number.
sqlEquivalent: UPDATE books SET price = price + 2

text: Add 'bestseller' to the tags array of the book titled '1984':
explanation: $push appends an element to an array field. If the field doesn't exist, MongoDB creates it. To add multiple elements at once, use { $push: { tags: { $each: ['a', 'b'] } } }.
hint: Push something onto an array.
sqlEquivalent: No direct SQL equivalent — would require INSERT into junction table

text: Apply a 10% discount (multiply price by 0.9) to all books published before the year 2000:
explanation: $lt means 'less than'. $mul multiplies the existing field value by the given number. { $mul: { price: 0.9 } } multiplies price by 0.9 (a 10% discount).
hint: $lt = less than. There's a multiply operator that starts with $mul.
sqlEquivalent: UPDATE books SET price = price * 0.9 WHERE published_year < 2000

text: What does the upsert option do in updateOne?
options: Updates multiple documents matching the filter
Inserts the document if no match is found, updates it if found
Returns the document before the update
Creates a copy of the document before updating
correctAnswer: Inserts the document if no match is found, updates it if found
explanation: Upsert = Update + Insert. If the filter matches a document, it's updated. If no match, a new document is created using the filter + update values.
hint: U + psert = Update if exists, Insert if not.
sqlEquivalent: INSERT INTO books ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE ...

text: Return only books with price < 13, showing only title and price (no _id). Fill in the projection:
explanation: In the projection object, set fields to 1 to include them. Set _id: 0 to exclude the automatic _id. You cannot mix includes and excludes except for _id.
hint: The two fields you want to return.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT title, price FROM books WHERE price < 13

text: Delete all books where inStock is false. Fill in the method name:
explanation: deleteMany() removes all documents matching the filter. deleteOne() only removes the first match.
hint: Delete ALL matching documents.
sqlEquivalent: DELETE FROM books WHERE in_stock = false

text:
This code is supposed to add a 'featured' field to the book '1984', but it has a bug. Find and fix it:

db.books.updateOne({ title: '1984' }, { featured: true })
explanation: Without $set, the update argument is treated as a replacement document — the entire book document would be replaced with { featured: true }, losing all other fields. Always use $set to update specific fields.
hint: You need an update operator to tell MongoDB you only want to SET one field.

text:
Translate this SQL to MongoDB:

SELECT title, authorName FROM books ORDER BY rating DESC LIMIT 5;
explanation: Projection selects fields (like SELECT columns), .sort({ rating: -1 }) = ORDER BY rating DESC, .limit(5) = LIMIT 5. Empty filter {} = no WHERE clause.
hint: -1 in sort = DESC in SQL.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT title, authorName FROM books ORDER BY rating DESC LIMIT 5

text: db.books.updateMany({ genres: 'fiction' }, { $set: { label: 'Fiction Classic' } }) — what does this return?
options: { acknowledged: true, matchedCount: N, modifiedCount: M }
correctAnswer: { acknowledged: true, matchedCount: N, modifiedCount: M }
explanation: Update operations return an UpdateResult with matchedCount (documents matching the filter) and modifiedCount (documents actually changed). modifiedCount can be less than matchedCount if some docs already had the new value.
hint: It tells you how many matched and how many were actually changed.

text: Use arrayFilters to increase the price of all order items where unitPrice > 10 by $2. Fill in the blanks:
explanation: $[item] is a positional filtered operator. The arrayFilters option defines conditions for the placeholder 'item'. This lets you update specific array elements based on their values.
hint: The arrayFilter condition references the placeholder name 'item' with a dot-path to the field.
sqlEquivalent: No clean SQL equivalent — would need a complex UPDATE with JSON functions in PostgreSQL

text: What is the difference between find() returning a cursor and findOne() returning a document in Node.js?
options: find() returns a lazy cursor; you must call .toArray() or iterate it. findOne() returns the document directly.
findOne() is faster because it uses an index; find() doesn't
find() returns a Promise; findOne() is synchronous
correctAnswer: find() returns a lazy cursor; you must call .toArray() or iterate it. findOne() returns the document directly.
explanation: In Node.js, the native driver's find() returns a FindCursor — a lazy iterator that doesn't execute the query until you consume it with .toArray(), .next(), or for-await-of. findOne() executes immediately and resolves to a single document or null.
hint: A cursor is like a stream — it doesn't pull all data at once.

explanation: The option is { upsert: true }, not { update: true }. upsert: true tells MongoDB to insert a new document if no match is found.
hint: The option name is a blend of 'update' and 'insert'.

text: What is the difference between bulk insertMany and individual insertOne calls in a loop?
options: No difference — MongoDB queues writes internally
insertMany sends one network round trip for all documents; individual insertOne calls each send a separate round trip
insertMany is atomic across all documents; insertOne calls are each atomic individually
insertMany validates schema; insertOne does not
correctAnswer: insertMany sends one network round trip for all documents; individual insertOne calls each send a separate round trip
explanation: insertMany batches all documents into a single network request, dramatically reducing latency for bulk inserts. A loop of 1000 insertOne calls would send 1000 round trips.
hint: Think about network round trips.
sqlEquivalent: INSERT INTO books VALUES (...),(...),(...) vs 1000 separate INSERT statements

text:
Translate this SQL:

UPDATE books SET in_stock = false WHERE published_year < 1950 AND price > 14;
explanation: Multiple fields in the same filter object = implicit AND. $lt = less than (<), $gt = greater than (>). Must use $set to avoid replacing the document.
hint: Multiple conditions in the same filter object are AND-ed automatically.
sqlEquivalent: UPDATE books SET in_stock = false WHERE published_year < 1950 AND price > 14

text: Write a query to find all books by 'Haruki Murakami' sorted by rating descending, returning only title and rating (exclude _id).
expectedQuery: db.books.find({ authorName: 'Haruki Murakami' }, { title: 1, rating: 1, _id: 0 }).sort({ rating: -1 })
explanation: Filter by authorName, project only title + rating (excluding _id with _id: 0), and sort by rating -1 (descending).
hint: Combine find with projection and sort.
sqlEquivalent: SELECT title, rating FROM books WHERE author_name = 'Haruki Murakami' ORDER BY rating DESC

text: Write a query to remove the 'promoCode' field from ALL books that currently have a null promoCode (use $exists and $unset).
explanation: Filter { promoCode: null } matches documents where promoCode is null. $unset removes the field entirely rather than setting it to null.
hint: $unset removes a field from the document.

text: You want to use skip/limit for pagination but performance degrades badly on later pages. Why?
options: skip() must read and discard all N preceding documents even with an index — gets slower as skip increases
limit() is not supported with indexes
Pagination requires MongoDB Atlas, not local MongoDB
correctAnswer: skip() must read and discard all N preceding documents even with an index — gets slower as skip increases
explanation: skip(1000) makes MongoDB read 1001 documents and discard the first 1000. For large offsets, use range-based pagination instead: store the last _id seen and query { _id: { $gt: lastSeenId } }.limit(pageSize).
hint: skip has to 'walk through' all the documents it's skipping.
sqlEquivalent: Same problem exists in SQL with OFFSET — PostgreSQL also degrades with large OFFSET values

text: findOneAndUpdate() vs updateOne() — what is the key difference?
options: findOneAndUpdate is faster because it uses findOne internally
findOneAndUpdate returns the document (before or after update); updateOne only returns match/modified counts
updateOne supports arrayFilters; findOneAndUpdate does not
findOneAndUpdate is atomic; updateOne is not
correctAnswer: findOneAndUpdate returns the document (before or after update); updateOne only returns match/modified counts
explanation: findOneAndUpdate returns the document itself. By default it returns the pre-update version; pass { returnDocument: 'after' } to get the updated document. Use updateOne when you only care about whether the update succeeded, not the document contents.
hint: One returns the document, one returns metadata.
sqlEquivalent: UPDATE ... RETURNING * in PostgreSQL (findOneAndUpdate) vs UPDATE (updateOne)

text: What is the most efficient way to delete ALL documents from a collection while keeping the collection itself (indexes, etc.)?
options: Both deleteMany({}) and drop() are equivalent
explanation: deleteMany({}) removes all documents but keeps the collection structure and indexes. drop() removes the entire collection including indexes. Use drop() only if you want to start completely fresh.
hint: drop() is more destructive — it removes the collection itself.
sqlEquivalent: TRUNCATE TABLE books (deleteMany) vs DROP TABLE books (drop)
